questions.prompt: What is the frequency response of a system according to the note?
questions.explanation: The note defines the frequency response by evaluating the transfer function on the imaginary axis, so H(omega)=H(s)|_{s=j omega}.

options: The amplitude scaling of the sinusoidal response
The time delay in seconds only
questions.correctAnswer: The amplitude scaling of the sinusoidal response
questions.explanation: The note calls |H(omega)| the amplitude response because it tells how strongly the output sinusoid is scaled at each frequency.

options: The phase shift of the sinusoidal response
questions.correctAnswer: The phase shift of the sinusoidal response
questions.explanation: The phase response is the angle of H(omega), and it tells how much the output sinusoid is shifted relative to the input.

questions.prompt: Which expression converts magnitude response to decibels?
questions.explanation: The lecture note explicitly writes the Bode magnitude in dB as 20 log10 |H(omega)|.

questions.prompt: If the dB magnitude is greater than 0 dB, the system is:
questions.explanation: The note directly states that positive dB corresponds to amplification, meaning the output amplitude is larger than the input amplitude.

questions.prompt: If the dB magnitude is less than 0 dB, the system is:
questions.explanation: Negative dB means the magnitude ratio is below one, so the system attenuates that frequency component.

options: a magnitude plot and a phase plot against frequency
a pole plot and a root-locus plot
an impulse response and a step response only
questions.correctAnswer: a magnitude plot and a phase plot against frequency
questions.explanation: The note uses Bode plots to mean the two frequency-domain graphs: magnitude versus omega and phase versus omega.

questions.prompt: Why is the substitution s=j omega used in frequency response?
options: Because steady sinusoids are represented on the imaginary axis
Because all stable poles lie on the imaginary axis
Because the phase response becomes zero
questions.correctAnswer: Because steady sinusoids are represented on the imaginary axis
questions.explanation: The imaginary axis is the axis associated with sustained sinusoidal factors, so evaluating H(s) there gives the sinusoidal steady-state response.

questions.prompt: How can Bode information be obtained experimentally according to the note?
options: Apply different sinusoidal input frequencies and measure output amplitude and phase
Apply only a step input and read the answer directly
questions.correctAnswer: Apply different sinusoidal input frequencies and measure output amplitude and phase
questions.explanation: The note says frequency response can be built experimentally by varying the input sinusoid frequency and observing amplitude gain and phase shift.

questions.prompt: Which statement best matches the role of frequency response in this lesson?
options: It describes how the system treats each sinusoidal input frequency
It replaces the transfer function completely
It only applies to unstable systems
It is unrelated to steady-state response
questions.correctAnswer: It describes how the system treats each sinusoidal input frequency
questions.explanation: This lesson uses frequency response as a direct description of how each sinusoidal frequency is amplified and phase shifted in steady state.
